title:
Clinical Scanning
Workflow
subtitle: Redesigning the clinical scanning workflow for speed, clarity, and decision-making in real treatment conditions.

slides.title:
Clinical Scanning
Workflow
slides.description:
The patient is in the chair. The scanner is ready. The system isn't.
I redesigned Align Technology's RX, Scan, and View workflow into one continuous system.

metaItems.label: Role
metaItems.value: [ADD: your role — e.g. Lead Product Designer]

slides.label: The System
slides.title: A system used during live patient treatment
slides.content: iTero is used chair-side. Three phases — RX, Scan, View — feed into each other. A problem in one disrupts the whole appointment.
issues: RX → define patient, procedure, case details
Scan → capture a real-time 3D model
View → validate before lab submission
slides.highlight: Every decision happens under time pressure — with the patient in the chair.

slides.label: The Problem
slides.title: The system grew. The experience didn't.
slides.content: RX, Scan, and View grew independently over time — three disconnected systems dressed up as one workflow.
issues: Setup blocked users before they could scan
Procedure and tooth selection took too much effort
Scanning stopped every time a tool needed adjusting
Nothing signalled when a scan was complete
slides.highlight: A bad scan means rescanning — with the patient still in the chair.

slides.label: User Research
slides.title: What I heard in the room
slides.content: Chair-side observations and follow-up interviews with clinicians. The same frustrations, everywhere — accepted as part of the job. [ADD: participant count and research scope — e.g. N clinicians across N sessions]

quotes.text: The patient popup interrupts me before I even start.
quotes.author: Dr. Yael Levi

quotes.text: I change the procedure multiple times before I even begin scanning.
quotes.author: Dr. Amir Cohen

quotes.text: The moment I go looking for a tool, I lose my rhythm completely.
quotes.author: Dr. Noa Ben-David

quotes.text: I never fully trust the result the first time I see it.
quotes.author: Dr. Efrat Mizrahi

quotes.text: Even when I'm done, I keep going back to check.
quotes.author: Dr. Daniel Katz

quotes.text: I never know if I'm selecting the right tooth.
quotes.author: Dr. Lior Haddad

slides.label: User Flow Mapping
slides.title: Mapping the flow before touching the design
slides.content: Before designing, I mapped the full RX→Scan→View sequence as users lived it. The friction clusters were impossible to ignore.
issues: RX setup blocked the clinician before any scan began
Tool access broke the clinician's rhythm mid-scan
No completion signal — View felt unfinished
slides.highlight: The friction wasn't random — it lived at every phase handoff.

slides.label: Design Goals
slides.title: Four problems. Four things to get right.

goals.title: Start without friction
goals.description: Remove the setup gates that block users before scanning.

goals.title: Keep scanning unbroken
goals.description: Stop the interface from interrupting the act of scanning.

goals.title: Make every tool self-explanatory
goals.description: Clarity built in — not figured out over time.

goals.title: Make results trustworthy on first view
goals.description: A clear signal that the scan is done — no re-checks.

kpis: Time from RX entry to first scan action

slides.label: Interactive Prototype
slides.title: Testing decisions before they became real
slides.content: I built an interactive prototype in code to simulate the real workflow before development started. Static screens can't capture continuous motion or tool sequencing — this could.
bullets2: Real workflow simulation — not static screens
slides.highlight: I caught real issues here: the exact moment a scan loses rhythm, the tool reach that breaks focus. None of that shows up on a static frame.

slides.title: RX
slides.subtitle: Defining the patient, procedure, and case before scanning — and the setup gate I had to clear first.

slides.label: RX Setup — Before
slides.title: Setup blocked the scan before it could start
slides.content: The old product was a dense, multi-step form — patient and procedure gated behind a blocking modal before scanning could begin.
issues: Patient selection required a multi-step modal
Procedure had to be defined upfront
Dropdown disconnected from the dental chart
slides.highlight: Every second here was a second the patient sat waiting.
slides.caption: Old RX setup — the blocking multi-step form. (Add img-uykask3eo.webp)

slides.label: RX Setup — Exploration
slides.title: Three approaches to RX setup
slides.dir1Desc: A collapse/expand layout — tap to open, read, close, repeat. In practice it meant constantly reopening the same section. That friction alone killed it.
slides.dir2Desc: Broke setup into a step-by-step wizard — organised, but more clicks and a slower start.
slides.dir3Desc: Everything visible at once — no expanding, no steps. The full setup readable in a single view.

slides.label: RX Setup — After
slides.title: Users start scanning right away
slides.content: Users start right away — patient context fills in as they go, not as a prerequisite.
issues: Inline patient selection — no modal
Patient context stays visible throughout
Procedure selection tied to teeth structure
slides.caption: New RX setup — single-view, no blocking form. (Add vid-aouv40ns6o0.mp4)

slides.label: RX Tooth Selection
slides.title: Selecting teeth should be as fast as pointing at them

beforeImage.caption: Old — one modal per tooth, no overview

slides.beforeDescription: Every tooth needed its own modal — click, confirm, close, repeat. No overview of what you'd already done.
slides.afterDescription: Teeth selected directly on the chart — one or many at once — with procedures assigned inline.
beforeBullets: One blocking modal per tooth — no multi-select
No color coding — no clinical overview
afterBullets: Select one or many teeth in one interaction
Color-coded procedures visible on the chart
Full treatment plan visible without opening anything

slides.title: Scan & View
slides.subtitle: Capturing and validating a 3D model before lab submission — starting with where the tools should live.

slides.label: Scan Toolbar — Before
slides.title: Tools scattered, with no home
slides.content: Icons built up from different sources, no shared style — and no consistent place to find them.
issues: No shared visual language across icons
No dedicated place for tools
Every new feature added more scatter
slides.caption: Old toolbar — scattered, mixed-style icons. (Add img-c1djiu0kh0.webp — DESIGNER: confirm this image shows icon fragmentation, else supply a real old-toolbar screenshot.)

slides.label: Toolbar — Exploration
slides.title: Where should the tools live?
slides.dir1Desc: A fixed left rail — always visible. In practice it ate horizontal scan space and narrowed the 3D view at the worst moment.
slides.dir2Desc: A top bar anchored above the viewport. Tidier than the rail, but reaching up pulled focus away from the live scan.
slides.dir3Desc: One collapsible toolbar, fixed to one corner. Opens when needed, hides when not. I rebuilt the toolbar and procedure icons into one visual language as part of this — every tool reads at a glance.

slides.label: Scan Toolbar — After
slides.title: One toolbar, one visual language
slides.content: A unified icon set in a collapsible toolbar — one language, one home.
issues: Unified icon set — each maps to its action
One collapsible toolbar, always in one place
Scalable — new tools slot in without disruption
bullets2: Toolbar and procedure icons rebuilt into one visual system
Every tool reads at a glance
slides.caption: New collapsible toolbar + unified icon set. (Add vid-g2r58c0dp9s.mp4)

slides.label: Multi-scan & Compare
slides.title: Managing multiple scans  and comparing them
slides.content: Pre-treatment, post-treatment, and additional scans — organised by tabs, compared in a multi-layer panel.
bullets2: Add and switch between scans within one session
Overlay scans and adjust opacity in real time
slides.highlight: What used to be a mental task — holding two scans in your head — became a visual action. Overlay, adjust opacity, compare in seconds.

slides.label: View Tools
slides.title: From icon bars to clinical decision panels

beforeImage.caption: Old — unlabelled icon clusters, no hierarchy

afterImage.caption: New — named panels with one primary CTA per tool

slides.beforeDescription: Unlabelled icon clusters with no hierarchy — every meaning memorised, nothing self-explanatory.
slides.afterDescription: Named panels with one clear primary action — the same structure across every tool, nothing to memorise.
beforeBullets: No hierarchy — all actions equal weight
afterBullets: Named panels with one primary CTA per tool
AI detect is the default — review, not draw

slides.label: Outcomes
slides.title: What changed — and what I still need to measure

outcomes.title: Time to start scanning
outcomes.description: Setup removed — straight to scan. [ADD: real metric or post-launch quote if available]

outcomes.title: Interruptions mid-scan
outcomes.description: Fixed toolbar kept the rhythm. [ADD: real metric or post-launch quote if available]

outcomes.title: Tool clarity
outcomes.description: Named panels — each tells you what it does.

outcomes.title: Confidence in results
outcomes.description: One primary action per tool. No reason to re-check.

slides.highlight: Outcomes based on prototype testing and clinician sessions. [ADD: first real metric or post-launch quote once available]

slides.label: Lasting Impact
slides.title: The work became the system
slides.content: Redesign patterns became a design system — documented in Figma, built in code.
issues: Input states — default, filled, disabled, error
Button states — default, hover, pressed, disabled
Selection controls — checkbox, indeterminate, disabled
Progress states — uploading, processing, complete, failed
slides.highlight: These decisions became what every feature was built on.
slides.image: [ADD: design-system component image — use the original img-5pdk4g6ngg.webp]
slides.caption: Design system components formalised from the redesign

slides.label: Reflection
slides.title: What I'd do differently
whatWorked: Coded prototype — timing issues static screens never surface.
Mapping the full flow first — each goal had a clear owner.
whatFailed: Toolbar test came after early designs were reviewed.
[ADD: one more specific thing that didn't go as planned — a decision that needed more iteration, a constraint that changed late, a stakeholder dynamic]
whatYoudDoDifferently: Journey map in front of the team in week one.
[ADD: one concrete action — e.g. run the toolbar position test before wireframes, not alongside them]
slides.whatYouCouldntMeasure: Based on testing. [ADD: update with real post-launch data — rescan rate, setup time, satisfaction score]

slides.title: Thank You
slides.subtitle: One mental model. Three phases. Every clinician's rhythm intact.
